meta.description: The grammar of Adzmyst as spoken in the Vault of Heaven. Glyphs are semantic fields, not letters. Position determines function. Repetition is punctuation. The sacred seven are the grammatical skeleton.

positions.description: Every glyph in a set has a positional role. The same glyph in first position vs. last position carries different weight.

opening.name: Domain
opening.function: Sets the frame. What domain this word operates in. The subject, the origin, the invocation.
opening.rule: The opening glyph declares: this word is about X. Everything that follows operates within X's semantic field.

body.name: Action
body.function: The movement. The relationship. What happens between opening and closing. Modification, qualification, transformation.
body.rule: Body glyphs modify the domain. They are the verbs, the adjectives, the prepositions. Multiple body glyphs build complexity.

closing.name: Result
closing.function: Seals the word. What it becomes. Where it ends. The destination, the product, the signature.
closing.rule: The closing glyph declares: this word resolves to Y. A word without a closing glyph is open-ended — a question, a fragment, an invocation that awaits response.

repetition.description: Two or three identical glyphs in sequence function as punctuation, not vocabulary. They mark breath, pause, emphasis, or sealing.

doubling.name: Breath-mark
doubling.function: Intensifier or pause. Equivalent to a comma, colon, or caught breath. The glyph means itself, more so.

examples.𐤌𐤌: The deep, deepening. Chaos acknowledging itself.
examples.𐤎𐤎: The pillar, upheld. What stands is standing.
examples.𐤕𐤕: It is finished, finished. The seal affirmed.
examples.𐤀𐤀: Strength, renewed. The beginning beginning again.

doubling.rule: Doubling can occur at the end of any tier triads and above. It is most common in tetrads and pentads as a rhythmic close.

tripling.name: Seal-mark
tripling.function: Sacred seal. Equivalent to a full stop, an amen, a final signature. Rare. Only at the end of verses, oaths, and completions.

examples.𐤕𐤕𐤕: It is finished, it is finished, it is finished. The seal of seals. The word that ends the Book.

tripling.rule: Tripling occurs only in hexads and heptads, and only at the absolute end of a set. It marks the end of a sacred utterance.

sacredSeven.description: The seven glyphs that form ADZMYST are the grammatical skeleton. They do the structural work. The remaining 15 glyphs carry the specific content.

𐤀.name: Aleph
𐤀.function: Sets the domain of strength, origin, or beginning. Marks the start of a sequence, a lineage, or an invocation.
𐤀.preferredPosition: First or early in the body.
𐤀.semanticField: Ox · Strength · Beginning · The pull that draws the plow.
𐤀.whenClosing: As a closing glyph, Aleph means return to origin — the word circles back to its beginning.

𐤃.name: Dalet
𐤃.function: Marks a transition, a passage, a relationship between two states. The hinge of the word.
𐤃.preferredPosition: Middle. Never first in declarative forms; never last unless it IS the destination.
𐤃.semanticField: Door · Passage · Threshold · The moment between what was and what will be.
𐤃.specialRule: In threshold pairs, Dalet is always the first glyph. 𐤃 + X = the door to X.

𐤆.name: Zayin
𐤆.function: Marks a cut, a distinction, a separation. Divides the word into before and after.
𐤆.preferredPosition: Middle, between two things being divided.
𐤆.semanticField: Weapon · Division · The Cut · What separates the necessary from the excess.
𐤆.specialRule: Zayin rarely appears in opening or closing position. It is the knife that cuts the middle.

𐤌.name: Mem
𐤌.function: Marks depth, chaos, the unconscious, the unknown. Adds weight and mystery to the word.
𐤌.preferredPosition: Can appear anywhere. Common in opening (the deep as domain) and body (deepening the action).
𐤌.semanticField: Water · Chaos · The Deep · What flows beneath the surface.
𐤌.specialRule: Mem is the most common doubled glyph (𐤌𐤌). The deep contains the deep.

𐤉.name: Yod
𐤉.function: Marks action, making, the hand that shapes. The active principle in the word.
𐤉.preferredPosition: Middle or near the end — the hand that does, then seals.
𐤉.semanticField: Hand · Action · Making · What reaches out to shape the world.
𐤉.specialRule: Yod rarely opens. Action presupposes a domain to act upon.

𐤎.name: Samek
𐤎.function: Marks support, structure, what holds. Gives the word stability and permanence.
𐤎.preferredPosition: Middle or closing. A stabilizing force — or the final pillar.
𐤎.semanticField: Pillar · Support · What Holds · The structure that does not ask to be seen.
𐤎.specialRule: Samek is commonly doubled (𐤎𐤎). The pillar supports the pillar. Also appears in existential forms: 𐤎...𐤎 — what stands, stands.

𐤕.name: Taw
𐤕.function: Marks completion, signature, the seal. Closes the word and declares it finished.
𐤕.semanticField: Mark · Signature · Completion · What says: this is finished, this is true.
𐤕.specialRule: Taw is the only glyph that can double (𐤕𐤕) or triple (𐤕𐤕𐤕) as a seal. It is the most common closing glyph by far.

wordOrder.description: The default order is Domain → Action → Result. Order can shift for emphasis, mood, or ritual context.

declarative.description: States what is. The most common pattern. 'Strength, through X, reaches completion.'
declarative.example: 𐤀 𐤃 𐤕 — strength opens to completion

interrogative.description: Asks what lies beyond. The closing glyph is often omitted — the question remains open.
interrogative.example: 𐤄 𐤃 ? — what lies beyond the door?

imperative.description: Commands action. The hand acts, then opens a way. 'Do X, and pass through.'
imperative.example: 𐤉 𐤆 𐤃 — cut, and pass through

existential.description: Affirms what is. The pillar at both ends — what stands, stands. Used for oaths and eternal truths.
existential.example: 𐤎 𐤌 𐤎 — the deep stands

inversive.description: Reverses time. The end speaks the beginning. Used in prophecies, elegies, and recollections.
inversive.example: 𐤕 𐤃 𐤀 — from the end, a door to the beginning

tiers.description: Words are classified by glyph count. Each tier has grammatical properties and typical usage.

pair.description: Incomplete grammatical unit. A domain and its connector, or an action and its result — but not both. Used for inscriptions, door markings, quick signs.
pair.typicalStructure: Domain + Connector, or Action + Result, or Connector + Domain
pair.completeness: Partial. Requires context to be understood.

triad.description: The minimum complete unit. Domain + Action + Result, or a variation. Most everyday speech.

tetrad.description: The standard word length. Enough glyphs for nuance without excess. The natural form for concepts, names, and common inscriptions.

pentad.description: A formal or complex word. Used for titles, invocations, and ritual language.

hexad.description: A full statement. Laws, declarations, memorial inscriptions.
hexad.typicalStructure: Domain + Action + Action + Action + Action + Result
hexad.completeness: Complete and formal. Often carved in stone.

heptad.description: The sacred number. The rarest form. The full name ADZMYST is a heptad. Most heptads are variations on the sacred sequence.
heptad.typicalStructure: The seven sacred glyphs in some order — or six sacred plus one from the wider 22.
heptad.completeness: Complete and sealed. A heptad is never casual.
